nativeRange.habitat: Found on subtidal reef flats and in shallow protected lagoons, moving over sand, rubble, and coral patches.

decorPreferences: rockwork with caves and crevices for sleeping and retreat

diet.notes: Has been documented eating a wide range of items in the wild — algae, detritus, molluscs, crustaceans, worms, sea urchins, small fishes, corals, and tunicates. In captivity, generally advised to offer varied meaty foods (krill, squid, clams, mussels, small shrimp) alongside some marine algae. Hard-shelled foods help wear down the continuously growing teeth and reduce destructive chewing on tank decor.

imageCandidates.notes: Arpingstone; 1800x1416; tack-sharp full left-profile showing every diagnostic feature — yellow lips, blue lines below eye, black bars, white belly, yellow moustache line and dotted caudal-peduncle stripes; vivid, in-colour.
Rickard Zerpe; 1761x1321; sharp in-situ reef shot, complete side view, vivid coloration with clear blue eye lines and black bars on a live wild individual.
Mike Prince; 2048x1365; crisp full-body side profile against blue water, all markings clearly visible on a vibrant specimen.

summary: The Picasso Trigger is a boldly patterned Indo-Pacific triggerfish widely regarded as one of the more manageable triggerfish species to keep. It tends to be territorial and aggressive as it matures, and is not considered safe with ornamental invertebrates.
careNotes: The Picasso Trigger (Rhinecanthus aculeatus) is one of the most recognizable marine aquarium fishes, famous in Hawaii as the state fish 'Humuhumunukunukuapua'a'. It is generally considered one of the easier triggerfishes to keep, but tends to be aggressive and territorial — especially as it matures — and tends to bully smaller or more peaceful tankmates. Adults reach around 25–30 cm and need ample open swimming space; a 500 L (roughly 130+ US gallons) tank is a sensible long-term minimum, with larger volumes (700 L+) helping to dilute aggression and accommodate the species' restless cruising behavior. Rockwork should provide caves and crevices for sleeping but leave generous open swimming room. Reef compatibility is best approached with caution: specimens have been reported to mostly ignore stony corals but will readily consume ornamental shrimp, snails, tubeworms, and other small invertebrates, and may nip at clam mantles or rearrange aquascaping. Diet should include hard-shelled meaty foods (krill, clams in shell, small mussels, hermit crabs as treats) to wear down their continuously growing teeth — fish kept on soft foods can develop overgrown teeth that interfere with feeding. One per tank is generally advised unless the system is very large; conspecific aggression tends to be high. A secure lid is recommended, as triggerfish can occasionally jump when startled. The species is broadly considered hardy and disease-resistant when water quality is stable.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquariums. In the wild, males hold territories overlapping with 1–5 females in a haremic system and may maintain the same territory for years; females guard demersal egg masses laid in sand depressions and fan them with pectoral fins until they hatch the same evening, around sunset. Captive reproduction is generally limited to public aquaria with very large systems.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Hobby trade care sheet — sourced max size 10 in, 180 gal minimum, aggressive temperament, not reef-safe, temp 72-78F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025. Original assigned URL (liveaquaria.com root) was navigated to the species page via the Triggerfish collection.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic and wild-biology authority. Confirmed family Balistidae, order Tetraodontiformes, Indo-Pacific distribution (East Africa to Hawaii/Marquesas/Tuamotu, north to southern Japan), max length 30 cm TL, depth 0-50 m, temperature 24.5-29 C, omnivorous bottom feeder, haremic/polygynous reproduction with females guarding eggs.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Lagoon triggerfish)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~30 cm), Hawaiian state-fish status, territorial behavior, and haremic mating system. Confirmed alternate common names.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Hobby care guide — cross-checked size up to 12 in, temp 76-82 F (~24-28 C), pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.021-1.025, aggressive/territorial, lifespan ~10 years, easy-moderate care level. Source spread: LiveAquaria 180 gal (~681 L) vs Fishkeeping World 100 gal (~380 L) tank minimum; per playbook caution rule, used 500 L (rounded to allowed sizes, with the species' restless/territorial behavior justifying the larger end).

fish.breedingNotes: The species has been documented to reproduce in public aquaria but is not realistically bred in home systems. Males hold territories overlapping those of one or more females, and the female guards the egg mass after spawning.
